buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i],
			&die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);
finding.description: If abbrev->count is 0 (an unsigned type like size_t), then `abbrev->count - 1` wraps around to SIZE_MAX due to unsigned integer underflow. The loop condition `i < SIZE_MAX` is effectively always true, causing massive out-of-bounds reads and writes on `die->attr_values[i]` and `abbrev->defs[i]`.

code_snippets:
buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i],
			&die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);

step_by_step_execution.description: parse_die is called with an abbrev whose count field is 0.
The loop condition computes abbrev->count - 1. Since count is an unsigned type and equals 0, this underflows to SIZE_MAX.
The loop iterates with i starting at 0, and the condition i < SIZE_MAX is true, so the loop body executes.
memset and parse_attr_value access die->attr_values[i] and abbrev->defs[i] far beyond their allocated bounds, causing heap-based out-of-bounds writes.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-43814: when abbrev->count is 0, the unsigned subtraction wraps to SIZE_MAX, causing a massive out-of-bounds loop and heap-based OOB write. The fix adds an `if (abbrev->count)` guard to prevent this.

actual_issue_summary: When abbrev->count is 0, the expression `abbrev->count - 1` underflows (unsigned) to SIZE_MAX, causing the for loop to iterate far out of bounds, resulting in heap-based out-of-bounds writes on die->attr_values and abbrev->defs arrays. The fix adds a check `if (abbrev->count)` before the loop.

cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

finding.description: The buffer `drc_name` is not null-terminated after `memcpy`, but `strchr` is called on it, which scans memory until it finds the target character or a null terminator. Since `drc_name` is an uninitialized stack buffer and only `nbytes` bytes are copied (without a null terminator), `strchr` can read beyond the copied region into uninitialized stack memory. If no '\n' or '\0' happens to exist in the remaining uninitialized portion of the array (or beyond it), `strchr` will perform an out-of-bounds read past the `drc_name` array.

memcpy copies exactly nbytes bytes into uninitialized stack buffer drc_name without adding null terminator

state.value: nbytes bytes copied, rest is uninitialized stack data

step_by_step_execution.description: strchr scans drc_name for '\n', but since the buffer is not null-terminated, it reads past the copied region into uninitialized stack memory

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies a core component of CVE-2021-28972. The CVE is about the lack of proper null-termination after memcpy into drc_name, which causes strchr to scan into uninitialized stack memory. If strchr finds a '\n' in uninitialized memory beyond nbytes, the code then writes '\0' to that location, corrupting data on the stack. The finding accurately describes the read-side consequence (strchr reading uninitialized memory) of the same root cause that the CVE describes, and this directly enables the stack corruption the CVE warns about.

actual_issue_summary: The drc_name stack buffer is not null-terminated after memcpy, causing strchr to scan into uninitialized stack memory. If a '\n' byte is found in the uninitialized portion, writing '\0' there corrupts the kernel stack. The fix replaces memcpy with strscpy to ensure null-termination, and only replaces '\n' with '\0' when actually found in the copied string.

cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

finding.description: The pipe_buffer's `flags` field is never initialized when a new buffer entry is set up. This means `buf->flags` retains whatever value it had from a previous use of that pipe ring slot. If a prior operation (e.g., a splice or pipe read/write cycle) left `PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE` set on that slot, a subsequent `pipe_write()` call will see the flag and merge user-controlled data directly into the page cache page referenced by `buf->page`. This allows an unprivileged user to overwrite the contents of read-only files (the Dirty Pipe vulnerability, CVE-2022-0847).

step_by_step_execution.description: A new pipe_buffer is allocated in the while loop of push_pipe when the pipe is not full.

state.value: pointer to pipe->bufs[iter_head & p_mask], a previously used pipe ring slot

step_by_step_execution.description: buf->ops, buf->page, buf->offset, and buf->len are initialized, but buf->flags is NOT set.

step_by_step_execution.description: If the stale flags contain PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE, a subsequent pipe_write() will merge user data directly into the page cache page, allowing writes to read-only file pages.

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely describes CVE-2022-0847 (Dirty Pipe): the missing initialization of buf->flags in push_pipe allows stale PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE to persist, enabling unprivileged writes to read-only page cache pages. This matches the CVE description and the fix (adding buf->flags = 0) exactly.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2022-0847 (Dirty Pipe): The push_pipe() and copy_page_to_iter_pipe() functions fail to initialize the 'flags' member of newly allocated pipe_buffer structures. Stale PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE flags from previous use allow unprivileged users to write to page cache pages of read-only files, escalating privileges. The fix adds 'buf->flags = 0' initialization.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2009-0689 is an array index error in dtoa's Balloc() function where the parameter k is not validated against the freelist array bounds (Kmax). A large precision value in a printf format string can cause k to exceed Kmax, leading to out-of-bounds array access on freelist[], incorrect memory allocation via 1 << k overflow, and ultimately a heap-based buffer overflow. The fix adds a bounds check (k > Kmax) and a secondary NULL check on the MALLOC result.

cve_desc: QEMU, through version 2.10 and through version 3.1.0, is vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read of up to 128 bytes in the hw/i2c/i2c-ddc.c:i2c_ddc() function. A local attacker with permission to execute i2c commands could exploit this to read stack memory of the qemu process on the host.

finding.description: If s->reg is not properly bounded, repeated calls to i2c_ddc_rx will increment s->reg beyond the size of s->edid_blob, causing an out-of-bounds read. There is no bounds check on s->reg before indexing into s->edid_blob.

s->edid_blob is indexed by s->reg without bounds checking

Out-of-bounds value is returned to caller, leaking stack memory

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2019-3812: an out-of-bounds read due to s->reg not being bounded when indexing into s->edid_blob. The fix adds modular arithmetic to keep the index within bounds.

actual_issue_summary: The i2c_ddc_rx function reads from s->edid_blob[s->reg] without bounding s->reg to the size of edid_blob. Repeated calls increment s->reg past the buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read of up to 128 bytes of stack memory. The fix applies modulo sizeof(s->edid_blob) to the index.

cve_desc: A flaw was found in libvirt in the virConnectListAllNodeDevices API in versions before 7.0.0. It only affects hosts with a PCI device and driver that supports mediated devices (e.g., GRID driver). This flaw could be used by an unprivileged client with a read-only connection to crash the libvirt daemon by executing the 'nodedev-list' virsh command. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

finding.description: Operator precedence bug causes out-of-bounds memory access. The expression `*mdev_types[i]` is parsed as `*(mdev_types[i])` due to `[]` having higher precedence than unary `*`. The intended expression is `(*mdev_types)[i]`. When `i >= 1`, `mdev_types[i]` reads memory past the single `virMediatedDeviceTypePtr *` pointer that `mdev_types` points to, resulting in an out-of-bounds read (and then the dereferenced garbage value is passed to the free function, potentially causing a use-after-free or arbitrary free).

step_by_step_execution.description: virNodeDeviceGetMdevTypesCaps is called with mdev_types pointing to a pointer to an array of virMediatedDeviceTypePtr, and nmdev_types > 1.

state.value: pointer to a single virMediatedDeviceTypePtr* value

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop iterates with i starting at 0. For i=0, *mdev_types[0] is equivalent to *(mdev_types[0]) which equals *(*mdev_types), accessing the first element correctly.
For i=1, *mdev_types[1] is parsed as *(mdev_types[1]), which reads mdev_types[1] — memory beyond the single pointer that mdev_types points to — causing an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: out-of-bounds read past the mdev_types pointer

step_by_step_execution.description: The out-of-bounds value is dereferenced and passed to virMediatedDeviceTypeFree, leading to a crash (segfault) or potentially freeing arbitrary memory.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact bug fixed by the CVE: the operator precedence issue where `*mdev_types[i]` should be `(*mdev_types)[i]`. This causes out-of-bounds memory access leading to a crash, which matches the CVE description of a segfault in virConnectListAllNodeDevices.

actual_issue_summary: Operator precedence bug in virNodeDeviceGetMdevTypesCaps: `*mdev_types[i]` was parsed as `*(mdev_types[i])` instead of the intended `(*mdev_types)[i]`, causing out-of-bounds memory access and a segfault when iterating over mediated device types during a nodedev-list operation.

cve_desc: The ReadTGAImage function in coders\tga.c in ImageMagick 7.0.5-6 has a memory leak vulnerability that can cause memory exhaustion via invalid colors data in the header of a TGA or VST file.

finding.description: When a TGA file has colormap_type != 0 with colormap_index = 0 and colormap_length = 0, image->colors is set to 0. AcquireImageColormap is then called with 0 entries. During pixel reading in the 8-bit case, the code accesses image->colormap via ConstrainColormapIndex which may return index 0 on a zero-length colormap allocation, resulting in a heap out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: colormap_type != 0 with colormap_index=0 and colormap_length=0

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential out-of-bounds read with a zero-length colormap, which is a different issue from the CVE. The CVE (CVE-2017-11170) is about memory exhaustion caused by excessively large colormap allocation when colormap_type==0 and bits_per_pixel causes `one << bits_per_pixel` to produce a huge value. The fix adds a bounds check on image->colors, not on the colormap_type!=0 path.

finding.description: When image_type is TGAMonochrome (PseudoClass) with colormap_type = 0 and bits_per_pixel = 32, the expression 'one << 32' where 'one' is a size_t causes undefined behavior on platforms where size_t is 32 bits (shifting by the full width of the type). This could result in image->colors being 0, leading to a zero-size colormap allocation followed by out-of-bounds access, or other unexpected behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: bits_per_pixel = 32 causes shift overflow

judgments.reasoning: This finding identifies the exact line of code that the fix addresses (`image->colors=one << tga_info.bits_per_pixel`). The CVE is about memory exhaustion via invalid colors data in the TGA header, and the fix adds a bounds check on `image->colors` right after this line to prevent excessively large allocations. While the finding focuses on undefined behavior for 32-bit shifts specifically and the CVE description emphasizes memory exhaustion, both point to the same root cause: unchecked `image->colors` computation from `bits_per_pixel` leading to resource issues.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a memory exhaustion issue in ReadTGAImage. When colormap_type is 0 and image is PseudoClass, `image->colors` is computed as `1 << bits_per_pixel` without bounds checking. With large bits_per_pixel values (e.g., 16), this could allocate an enormous colormap, causing memory exhaustion. The fix adds a check that `image->colors` does not exceed `(~0UL)/sizeof(*image->colormap)` before proceeding with the colormap allocation.

cve_desc: In the Linux kernel before 5.3.6, there is a use-after-free bug that can be caused by a malicious USB device in the drivers/net/ieee802154/atusb.c driver, aka CID-7fd25e6fc035.

finding.description: ieee802154_free_hw() frees the hw object and its associated private data (the `atusb` struct itself, which was allocated via ieee802154_alloc_hw). After this call, `atusb` is a dangling pointer. The subsequent accesses `usb_set_intfdata(interface, NULL)` is fine (doesn't use atusb), but `usb_put_dev(atusb->usb_dev)` dereferences the already-freed `atusb` structure, resulting in a use-after-free.

state.value: valid pointer to atusb struct (private data of hw)

usb_put_dev(atusb->usb_dev) dereferences the freed atusb struct to access usb_dev, causing a use-after-free.

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely matches the CVE: ieee802154_free_hw frees the atusb struct, and the subsequent usb_put_dev(atusb->usb_dev) dereferences the freed memory. The fix moves usb_put_dev before ieee802154_free_hw, exactly addressing this issue.

actual_issue_summary: In atusb_disconnect, ieee802154_free_hw(atusb->hw) frees the atusb private data structure, but usb_put_dev(atusb->usb_dev) is called afterward, dereferencing the already-freed atusb pointer. The fix reorders the calls so usb_put_dev is called before ieee802154_free_hw.

cve_desc: Spice, versions 0.5.2 through 0.14.1, are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read due to an off-by-one error in memslot_get_virt. This may lead to a denial of service, or, in the worst case, code-execution by unauthenticated attackers.

Since valid indices are 0 to num_memslots_groups-1, using group_id == num_memslots_groups leads to an out-of-bounds array access on info->mem_slots[group_id].

finding.description: Off-by-one error in slot_id validation. The check uses '>' instead of '>=', allowing slot_id equal to info->num_memslots to pass. Since valid indices are 0 to num_memslots-1, using slot_id == num_memslots leads to an out-of-bounds array access on info->mem_slots[group_id][slot_id].

The check slot_id > num_memslots is false, so execution continues
Out-of-bounds access on info->mem_slots[group_id][slot_id]

cve_desc: The apprentice_load function in libmagic/apprentice.c in the Fileinfo component in PHP through 5.6.4 attempts to perform a free operation on a stack-based character array, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption or application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via unknown vectors.  NOTE: this is disputed by the vendor because the standard erealloc behavior makes the free operation unreachable

finding.description: When erealloc fails (returns NULL), the code calls efree(mfn) on a stack-allocated buffer. mfn is declared as `char mfn[MAXPATHLEN]` on the stack, but efree() is intended for heap-allocated memory. This results in freeing stack memory, which corrupts the heap allocator's metadata and can lead to undefined behavior, crashes, or exploitable conditions.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function declares mfn as a stack-based character array: `char mfn[MAXPATHLEN]`
The code enters the directory processing branch and iterates through directory entries, writing into mfn via snprintf

state.value: contains path string on the stack

step_by_step_execution.description: When files >= maxfiles, erealloc is called to resize filearr. If erealloc returns NULL, the error handling path is entered.
In the error path, efree(mfn) is called on the stack-allocated buffer, attempting to free stack memory

state.value: stack-allocated array being passed to efree (heap free)

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches the CVE and the diff. CVE-2014-9426 describes an attempted free operation on a stack-based character array in apprentice_load, and the fix removes the `efree(mfn)` call. The finding correctly identifies that mfn is stack-allocated and efree is called on it in the erealloc failure path.

actual_issue_summary: The apprentice_load function calls efree(mfn) in an error path where mfn is a stack-allocated char array (char mfn[MAXPATHLEN]), not heap-allocated memory. The fix removes the efree(mfn) call.

cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Heap-based Buffer Overflow
diff:
--- vulnerable
+++ fixed
@@ -45,12 +45,13 @@
 	p = NameBuff;
 	len = (int)STRLEN(p);
 
-	if (bt_help(wp->w_buffer)
+	if ((bt_help(wp->w_buffer)
 #ifdef FEAT_QUICKFIX
-		|| wp->w_p_pvw
+		    || wp->w_p_pvw
 #endif
-		|| bufIsChanged(wp->w_buffer)
-		|| wp->w_buffer->b_p_ro)
+		    || bufIsChanged(wp->w_buffer)
+		    || wp->w_buffer->b_p_ro)
+		&& len < MAXPATHL - 1)
 	    *(p + len++) = ' ';
 	if (bt_help(wp->w_buffer))
 	{

finding.description: When the translated buffer name stored in NameBuff is close to MAXPATHL in length, the unconditional write `*(p + len++) = ' '` can write past the end of the NameBuff buffer without any bounds check. Additionally, the null terminator is not placed after this write, so subsequent STRLEN calls may read out of bounds. The vim_snprintf calls that follow use `MAXPATHL - len` as the size argument, which can become zero or wrap to a large value if len >= MAXPATHL, failing to null-terminate the string.

step_by_step_execution.description: get_trans_bufname fills NameBuff with the buffer name, potentially up to MAXPATHL-1 characters.
The condition checks bt_help, w_p_pvw, bufIsChanged, or b_p_ro — if any is true, a space is written at p+len without checking if len < MAXPATHL-1.
*(p + len++) = ' ' writes one byte past the allocated NameBuff buffer (size MAXPATHL), causing a heap-based buffer overflow.
Subsequent STRLEN(p + len) may read out of bounds since the written space overwrote outside the buffer and no null terminator was placed.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact vulnerability fixed by the commit: the missing length check `len < MAXPATHL - 1` before writing a space character into NameBuff. The fix adds this bounds check. This matches the CVE-2022-0213 heap-based buffer overflow.

actual_issue_summary: When the buffer name in NameBuff is close to MAXPATHL in length, appending a space via `*(p + len++) = ' '` can write past the end of NameBuff (a heap-based buffer overflow). The fix adds a `len < MAXPATHL - 1` check before the write.

cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11.

finding.description: The `control_bytes` pointer is initialized to a position within the buffer and is subsequently incremented (via `control_bytes++`) every time a tag with `control_byte == 1` is encountered. There is no bounds check ensuring that `control_bytes` remains within the valid buffer region. If the parsed data (tagx) specifies multiple tags with `control_byte == 1`, the pointer can advance past the end of the buffer data, and a subsequent read of `control_bytes[0]` results in a heap-based out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: control_bytes pointer is set from buffer
control_bytes is incremented without bounds check
Out-of-bounds read occurs

judgments.reasoning: The CVE fix addresses a buffer over-read related to the index entry label: the `text` array was too small by 1 byte (`INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX` instead of `INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX + 1`), and there was no error checking after `mobi_indx_get_label` with corrupt input. The finding describes a different potential issue with the `control_bytes` pointer, which is unrelated to the actual CVE fix.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability was a buffer over-read (CWE-125) caused by two issues: (1) the `text` buffer was declared as `char text[INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX]` instead of `char text[INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX + 1]`, potentially missing space for the null terminator, and (2) there was no error checking after calling `mobi_indx_get_label()`, meaning corrupt input could cause a buffer read error that went undetected, leading to use of improperly terminated label data.

cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

finding.description: The memcpy copies a fixed `sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)` bytes from the packet buffer regardless of `hdr->length`. If `hdr->length` is smaller than `sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)`, the length check `(pnt + hdr->length + 3) > end` will pass, but the memcpy will read past the validated boundary, causing an out-of-bounds heap read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function receives a BGP capability message buffer with pnt and length.

state.value: pointer to start of capability message data
total length of the capability message

step_by_step_execution.description: The initial check ensures at least 3 bytes (action + capability header) are available.
hdr->length is read from the packet. An attacker sets hdr->length to a small value, e.g. 0.
The capability length check passes because pnt + 0 + 3 <= end.
memcpy copies sizeof(struct capability_mp_data) bytes from pnt+3, which exceeds the validated region since hdr->length < sizeof(struct capability_mp_data).

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-37032: an out-of-bounds read in bgp_capability_msg_parse due to memcpy reading sizeof(struct capability_mp_data) bytes without verifying hdr->length is at least that size. The fix adds the missing check.

actual_issue_summary: In bgp_capability_msg_parse, there was no check that hdr->length was at least sizeof(struct capability_mp_data) before the memcpy that copies that many bytes. When hdr->length is smaller, the boundary check passes but memcpy reads beyond validated data, causing an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125) leading to potential segfault/DoS.

cve_desc: Multiple integer overflows in the read_bitmap_file_data function in io-xbm.c in gdk-pixbuf before 2.26.1 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) via a negative (1) height or (2) width in an XBM file, which triggers a heap-based buffer overflow.

finding.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `size = bytes_per_line * hh`. Both `ww` and `hh` are parsed from attacker-controlled file input as unsigned integers. A crafted bitmap file can specify large width and height values such that their product overflows a 32-bit int, resulting in a small `g_malloc` allocation. The subsequent loop then writes far more data than was allocated, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Width and height are parsed from the XBM file via sscanf into `value`, then cast to unsigned int for `ww` and `hh`.
`bytes_per_line` is computed as `(ww+7)/8 + padding`.
`size = bytes_per_line * hh` overflows the 32-bit int, producing a small or negative value.

state.value: small or negative due to overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: `g_malloc(size)` allocates a small buffer based on the overflowed size.
The loop writes `size` (pre-overflow logical count) bytes into the small buffer, causing heap overflow.

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the integer overflow in the `size = bytes_per_line * hh` multiplication, which is one of the two issues fixed in the commit (the other being negative width/height). The diff adds an explicit overflow check `if (size / bytes_per_line != hh)` for exactly this issue. The CVE description mentions 'multiple integer overflows' and the finding captures this component. Note that negative width/height values (cast to large unsigned values) are one way to trigger this overflow, making the finding directly related to CVE-2012-2370.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2012-2370 involves two related issues in the XBM loader: (1) negative width or height values from the file are cast to large unsigned integers without validation, and (2) the multiplication `bytes_per_line * hh` can overflow, leading to a small allocation followed by a heap buffer overflow. The fix rejects non-positive width/height values and adds an overflow check on the size computation.

cve_desc: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command in bookmarking function of Newsbeuter versions 0.7 through 2.9 allows remote attackers to perform user-assisted code execution by crafting an RSS item that includes shell code in its title and/or URL.

finding.description: The title, description, and feed_title parameters are passed through stfl::quote (an STFL UI escaping function, not a shell escaping function) and quote_empty, then interpolated into a shell command line. An attacker who controls RSS feed titles or descriptions can inject arbitrary shell commands. For example, a feed title containing $(malicious_command) or backtick-enclosed commands would be executed by /bin/sh. The url parameter is partially protected by replacing single quotes and wrapping in single quotes, but the other three parameters lack proper shell escaping.

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the CVE-2017-12904 vulnerability: the title, description, and feed_title parameters are not properly shell-escaped before being interpolated into a command line passed to /bin/sh, allowing OS command injection via crafted RSS items. The fix replaces the inadequate stfl::quote/quote_empty with proper single-quote wrapping and quote replacement, exactly matching what the finding describes.

actual_issue_summary: The bookmark function improperly sanitizes the title, description, and feed_title parameters before interpolating them into a shell command. These parameters used stfl::quote (a UI escaping function) instead of proper shell escaping, allowing an attacker to craft RSS items with shell metacharacters in titles/descriptions to achieve remote code execution.

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4436.

vim_strsave for new_line returns NULL
Function returns without freeing orig_line

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about a heap-based buffer overflow (CWE-787) caused by the while loop advancing new_cursor_col past the end of the line without checking for NUL terminator. The fix adds a check `if (ptr[new_cursor_col] == NUL) break;`. This finding describes a memory leak (CWE-401) which is an entirely different issue unrelated to the CVE.

cve_desc: ntpq in NTP 4.2.x before 4.2.8p4, and 4.3.x before 4.3.77 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via crafted mode 6 response packets.

finding.description: If `atoascii` fills `bv` to its full capacity (`sizeof(bv)` which is `2 * MAXVALLEN`), the null terminator will be at index `2 * MAXVALLEN - 1`. Then `strlen(bv)` returns `2 * MAXVALLEN - 1`, and the write `bv[len+1] = '\0'` writes at index `2 * MAXVALLEN`, which is one byte past the end of the `bv` buffer. This is a stack-based off-by-one buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: nextvar parses a crafted mode 6 response packet, extracting name and value strings

state.value: attacker-controlled string from crafted mode 6 response

atoascii is called with value, MAXVALLEN, bv, sizeof(bv) which is 2*MAXVALLEN, filling bv to full capacity

state.value: fully filled, null terminator at index 2*MAXVALLEN-1

bv[len] = output_raw writes at index 2*MAXVALLEN-1 (valid, overwrites null terminator)

actual_issue_summary: In ntpq's cookedprint(), when processing crafted mode 6 response packets, atoascii could fill bv to its full capacity, and then appending output_raw and a null terminator would write one byte past the end of the stack buffer bv. The fix reserves one byte of space (sizeof(bv)-1) when the output_raw character will be appended.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming STUN message contains an ERROR-CODE attribute, the header length is not checked before performing a subtraction operation, potentially resulting in an integer underflow scenario. This issue affects all users that use STUN. A malicious actor located within the victim’s network may forge and send a specially crafted UDP (STUN) message that could remotely execute arbitrary code on the victim’s machine. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

finding.description: The attribute header length (`attr->hdr.length`) is read directly from the attacker-controlled network buffer without validating that it is at least 4. If `attr->hdr.length` is less than 4, the subtraction `attr->hdr.length - 4` causes an integer underflow, resulting in a very large (or negative) `value.slen`. This is then passed to `pj_strdup`, which can cause a heap buffer over-read and potentially a massive heap allocation or crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: A STUN message with an ERROR-CODE attribute is received, where `attr->hdr.length` is less than 4 (e.g., 0).
The subtraction `attr->hdr.length - 4` underflows, producing a negative value for `value.slen`.
`pj_strdup` is called with the negative/underflowed length, leading to buffer over-read or other memory corruption.

state.value: negative, interpreted as very large unsigned value in memory operations

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly matches CVE-2021-37706: the integer underflow in `value.slen = attr->hdr.length - 4` when `hdr.length < 4`, which is exactly what the fix addresses by clamping `value.slen` to 0 when negative.

actual_issue_summary: In `decode_errcode_attr`, when an incoming STUN ERROR-CODE attribute has a header length less than 4, the computation `value.slen = attr->hdr.length - 4` results in an integer underflow (negative value). This is passed to `pj_strdup`, potentially causing a heap buffer over-read or arbitrary code execution. The fix clamps `value.slen` to 0 when negative.

cve_desc: A flaw was found in X.Org Server before xorg-x11-server 1.20.9. An Integer underflow leading to heap-buffer overflow may lead to a privilege escalation vulnerability. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to data confidentiality and integrity as well as system availability.

finding.description: Integer underflow in dataLeft when processing events with size=1 (XkbBellNotify, XkbActionMessage, XkbCompatMapNotify). The length check uses the original size (1), verifying dataLeft >= 2. However, the else branch then sets size=2, causing the pointer to advance by 4 and dataLeft to be decremented by 4. If dataLeft is 2 or 3, the unsigned subtraction wraps around to a huge value, allowing subsequent loop iterations to read/write out-of-bounds memory (swapping bytes beyond the request buffer).

step_by_step_execution.description: dataLeft is computed from stuff->length
size=1 event type is processed, check dataLeft >= size*2 = 2 passes
else branch sets size=2
from.c8 advances by 4, dataLeft -= 4 underflows
subsequent iterations read/write out of bounds

judgments.reasoning: The finding describes a potential issue with the size=1/size=2 logic in the else branch, but the actual CVE (CVE-2020-14361) is about using the client-controlled `stuff->length` instead of the validated `client->req_len` to compute `dataLeft`. An attacker can supply a small `stuff->length` value (after byte-swapping) that causes `(stuff->length * 4) - SIZEOF(xkbSelectEventsReq)` to underflow, resulting in an enormous `dataLeft` that bypasses all length checks. The fix replaces `stuff->length` with `client->req_len`. The finding identifies the wrong root cause of the integer underflow.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in disable_priv_mode in shell.c in GNU Bash through 5.0 patch 11. By default, if Bash is run with its effective UID not equal to its real UID, it will drop privileges by setting its effective UID to its real UID. However, it does so incorrectly. On Linux and other systems that support "saved UID" functionality, the saved UID is not dropped. An attacker with command execution in the shell can use "enable -f" for runtime loading of a new builtin, which can be a shared object that calls setuid() and therefore regains privileges. However, binaries running with an effective UID of 0 are unaffected.

finding.description: When setuid() fails (and EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE is not defined, or the error is not EAGAIN), the function logs an error but continues execution. It then unconditionally updates current_user.euid and current_user.egid to the unprivileged values, causing the program to believe privileges were successfully dropped when they were not. The process continues running with elevated privileges while the internal state indicates otherwise.

Error is logged but execution continues
Internal state is updated to claim privileges were dropped

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about setuid() succeeding but failing to drop the saved UID, not about setuid() failing. The fix replaces setuid() with setresuid() to also reset the saved UID. This finding describes a different (minor) issue about error handling on setuid failure.

finding.description: setuid() is called before setgid(). If the process is running as root and setuid() succeeds in dropping to a non-root uid, the subsequent setgid() call may fail because the process no longer has the privilege to change its group ID. The correct order is to call setgid() first, then setuid().

step_by_step_execution.description: setuid() drops to non-root uid first
setgid() fails because process no longer has root privileges

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is specifically about setuid() not dropping the saved UID on systems with saved UID support, allowing privilege re-escalation. This finding describes a different concern about the ordering of setuid/setgid calls, which is not what the CVE or the fix addresses. The fix replaces setuid/setgid with setresuid/setresgid but does not change their ordering.

actual_issue_summary: The disable_priv_mode() function used setuid() and setgid() to drop privileges, but these calls do not reset the saved UID/GID on Linux and similar systems. An attacker could regain privileges by calling setuid() from a dynamically loaded builtin. The fix uses setresuid() and setresgid() to explicitly set the real, effective, and saved UIDs/GIDs all to the unprivileged values.

cve_desc: gio/gsocketclient.c in GNOME GLib 2.59.2 does not ensure that a parent GTask remains alive during the execution of a connection-attempting enumeration, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (g_socket_client_connected_callback mishandling and application crash) via a crafted web site, as demonstrated by GNOME Web (aka Epiphany).

The fix removes the `data = NULL` pattern and instead uses `task_completed_or_cancelled()` checks and proper lifetime management of the GTask, which is exactly what the CVE describes.

cve_desc: The mkdir procedure of GNU Guile temporarily changed the process' umask to zero. Between these two calls, the umask is set to 0, meaning any other thread creating files in that window will do so with no permission mask applied, potentially creating world-readable/writable files.

umask(mask) restores the original umask, but the damage is done.

The fix removes the umask calls entirely, passing 0777 directly to mkdir and letting the kernel apply the umask atomically.

The fix removes the umask calls and passes 0777 directly to mkdir(), letting the kernel apply the umask atomically.

cve_desc: ASN.1 strings are represented internally within OpenSSL as an ASN1_STRING structure which contains a buffer holding the string data and a field holding the buffer length. This contrasts with normal C strings which are repesented as a buffer for the string data which is terminated with a NUL (0) byte. Although not a strict requirement, ASN.1 strings that are parsed using OpenSSL's own "d2i" functions (and other similar parsing functions) as well as any string whose value has been set with the ASN1_STRING_set() function will additionally NUL terminate the byte array in the ASN1_STRING structure. However, it is possible for applications to directly construct valid ASN1_STRING structures which do not NUL terminate the byte array by directly setting the "data" and "length" fields in the ASN1_STRING array. This can also happen by using the ASN1_STRING_set0() function. Numerous OpenSSL functions that print ASN.1 data have been found to assume that the ASN1_STRING byte array will be NUL terminated, even though this is not guaranteed for strings that have been directly constructed. Where an application requests an ASN.1 structure to be printed, and where that ASN.1 structure contains ASN1_STRINGs that have been directly constructed by the application without NUL terminating the "data" field, then a read buffer overrun can occur. The same thing can also occur during name constraints processing of certificates (for example if a certificate has been directly constructed by the application instead of loading it via the OpenSSL parsing functions, and the certificate contains non NUL terminated ASN1_STRING structures). It can also occur in the X509_get1_email(), X509_REQ_get1_email() and X509_get1_ocsp() functions. If a malicious actor can cause an application to directly construct an ASN1_STRING and then process it through one of the affected OpenSSL functions then this issue could be hit. This might result in a crash (causing a Denial of Service attack). It could also result in the disclosure of private memory contents (such as private keys, or sensitive plaintext). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1l (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1k). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2za (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2y).

finding.description: The code checks that `params->base->data` is non-NULL but does not verify that `params->base->length` is greater than zero before accessing `params->base->data[0]`. An attacker can craft an ECPARAMETERS structure where `params->base` has a non-NULL `data` pointer but `length` of 0, leading to an out-of-bounds read when accessing `data[0]`.

This is the specific instance of CVE-2021-3712 in this function — reading from an ASN1_STRING's data buffer without ensuring the length is non-zero, causing a read buffer overrun (CWE-125).

When an ASN1_STRING has a non-NULL data pointer but zero length, this results in an out-of-bounds read. The fix adds `|| params->base->length == 0` to the validation check.

cve_desc: MP4Box is a component of GPAC-2.0.0, which is a widely-used third-party package on RPM Fusion. When MP4Box tries to parse a MP4 file, it calls the function `diST_box_read()` to read from video. In this function, it allocates a buffer `str` with fixed length. However, content read from `bs` is controllable by user, so is the length, which causes a buffer overflow.

finding.description: The loop reads bytes from the bitstream into a stack buffer `str[1024]` without checking that `i` stays within bounds. If the bitstream contains more than 1023 non-null bytes before a null terminator, `i` will exceed 1023 and cause a stack buffer overflow.

The while(1) loop reads bytes one at a time from the bitstream into str[i], incrementing i each iteration.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-1441: the fixed-size 1024-byte stack buffer `str` can be overflowed because user-controlled bitstream content is read without bounds checking. The fix replaces the fixed buffer with a dynamically allocated buffer sized to s->size.

actual_issue_summary: In diST_box_read(), a fixed-size stack buffer of 1024 bytes is used to read user-controlled data from the bitstream in an unbounded loop, causing a stack buffer overflow when the input exceeds 1023 bytes. The fix dynamically allocates a buffer based on the box size.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalMaxPool` can be made to crash a TensorFlow process via a division by 0. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.description: The check `DCHECK_GT(output_size[i], 0)` is a debug-only assertion that is compiled away in release/optimized builds. If a pooling_ratio is very large relative to the input dimension, output_size[i] becomes 0. This leads to allocating a zero-sized output tensor and constructing Eigen maps with 0 columns, but GeneratePoolingSequence may still return sequences that cause the inner loops to compute offsets and access memory out of bounds.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue: when pooling_ratio is larger than the input dimension, output_size becomes 0, which is the same division-by-zero / crash vulnerability described in CVE-2022-21735. The fix adds exactly the missing validation (input_size[i] >= pooling_ratio_[i]) that the finding implies is needed.

actual_issue_summary: When pooling_ratio is higher than the input dimension size, output_size[i] becomes 0 via floor(input_size[i] / pooling_ratio_[i]), causing a division by zero or crash. The fix adds a validation check ensuring input_size[i] >= pooling_ratio_[i] for each dimension.

Users can pass negative values to cause a segmentation fault based denial of service. In multiple places throughout the code, one may compute an index for a write operation. However, the existing validation only checks against the upper bound of the array. Hence, it is possible to write before the array by massaging the input to generate negative values for `loc`. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

finding.description: The bounds check on `loc` only verifies `loc < output_elements` but does not verify `loc >= 0`. Since `loc` is computed via `std::inner_product` over group indices and output strides (all `int64_t`), a malicious sparse tensor with negative indices can produce a negative `loc`. A negative `loc` passes the `loc < output_elements` check but results in an out-of-bounds write when used as an index into `output_t`.

step_by_step_execution.description: User input is read into variable `loc`

state.value: negative value from std::inner_product with negative indices

step_by_step_execution.description: The bounds check only checks loc < output_elements, which passes for negative loc
output_t(loc) writes to a negative offset, causing out-of-bounds memory access

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-29208: the missing lower-bound check (loc >= 0) allows negative indices to bypass validation, leading to out-of-bounds writes. The fix adds `0 <= loc &&` to all five check sites.

actual_issue_summary: The EditDistance op's bounds checks on the computed index `loc` only verified `loc < output_elements` but not `loc >= 0`. Negative sparse tensor indices could produce a negative `loc` via `std::inner_product`, bypassing the check and causing out-of-bounds memory writes. The fix adds `0 <= loc &&` to all five validation sites.

cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

finding.description: Division by zero when `delay_den` is read from the fcTL chunk as 0 via `png_get_uint_16`. There is no check that `delay_den` is non-zero before computing `delay_num * 1000 / delay_den`.

step_by_step_execution.description: A malicious APNG file is provided with a valid PNG signature and IHDR chunk. delay_den is initialized to 10.
An fcTL chunk is read where delay_den field (at chunk.p + 30) is set to 0.
On the next fcTL or IEND chunk, processing_finish succeeds and bundle.duration is computed as delay_num * 1000 / delay_den, causing division by zero.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-36692: a divide-by-zero in DecodeImageAPNG when delay_den is 0. The fix adds `if (!delay_den) delay_den = 100;` which is precisely what the diff shows.

actual_issue_summary: When parsing an APNG fcTL chunk, delay_den could be read as 0 from the file. This value was used as a divisor in `bundle.duration = delay_num * 1000 / delay_den` without any zero-check, causing a divide-by-zero crash. The fix defaults delay_den to 100 when it is 0.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalAvgPoolGrad` does not consider cases where the input tensors are invalid allowing an attacker to read from outside of bounds of heap. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.description: The values read from row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor (in_row_start, in_col_start) are attacker-controlled and never validated to be non-negative or within the bounds of [0, in_rows) and [0, in_cols). While in_row_end/in_col_end are clamped to the max index, the start values are not. A negative in_row_start or in_col_start leads to a negative in_index, causing an out-of-bounds read/write on in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat. Similarly, a very large start value could also produce an out-of-bounds in_index.

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker provides a row_seq_tensor or col_seq_tensor containing negative values.
in_row_start is set to the negative value from the sequence tensor.
in_row_end is computed and clamped to in_max_row_index, but in_row_start remains negative.
The inner loop iterates from in_row_start to in_row_end, producing negative in_r values.
in_index is computed as (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c, which is negative.
coeffRef(d, in_index) accesses memory before the start of the allocated buffer, causing an out-of-bounds heap read/write.

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly matches the CVE. The fix adds checks that row and column sequence tensor values must not be negative, preventing out-of-bounds heap access via negative indices.

finding.description: If the attacker provides sequence tensor values such that in_row_start equals in_row_end+1, num_elements_in_pooling_cell could be zero, causing a floating-point division by zero.

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker crafts sequence tensor values so that in_row_start > in_row_end after clamping, making the pooling cell size zero.
Division by zero occurs when computing out_backprop_element / num_elements_in_pooling_cell.

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is specifically about out-of-bounds heap read due to missing negative value checks on sequence tensors. Division by zero is a separate issue not addressed by the fix and not described in the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The FractionalAvgPoolGrad implementation did not validate that row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor values are non-negative. Negative values in these tensors would produce negative indices (in_row_start, in_col_start), leading to out-of-bounds heap memory access. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks ensuring both row and column sequence tensor values are >= 0.

cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.3 was discovered to contain a stack overflow in the function njs_default_module_loader at /src/njs/src/njs_module.c. NOTE: multiple third parties dispute this report, e.g., the behavior is only found in unreleased development code that was not part of the 0.7.2, 0.7.3, or 0.7.4 release

finding.description: When `dir` is not NULL, the `length` variable is overwritten with `dir->length` (plus an optional trailing slash). The subsequent bounds check (`length > NJS_MAX_PATH`) therefore only validates the directory portion, not the combined `dir->length + trail + info->name.length`. The function then copies both `dir` and `info->name` into the stack buffer `src[NJS_MAX_PATH + 1]`, resulting in a stack buffer overflow when their combined length exceeds NJS_MAX_PATH.

step_by_step_execution.description: length is initialized to info->name.length
dir is not NULL, so length is overwritten (=) with dir->length instead of added (+=)
Bounds check only validates dir->length (+ trail) against NJS_MAX_PATH, missing info->name.length
Both dir and info->name are copied into stack buffer src, overflowing it

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly identifies the bug fixed by the commit: `length = dir->length` should be `length += dir->length`. This causes an incomplete bounds check leading to a stack buffer overflow, matching CVE-2022-29379 (CWE-787).

actual_issue_summary: In njs_module_path(), `length = dir->length` overwrites the initial `info->name.length` value instead of adding to it (`length += dir->length`). This means the NJS_MAX_PATH bounds check only considers the directory length, not the full combined path, allowing a stack buffer overflow when copying both directory and module name into the fixed-size stack buffer.

cve_desc: In Dovecot before 2.3.11.3, sending a specially formatted RPA request will crash the auth service because a length of zero is mishandled.

finding.description: When `p` equals `end`, the check `p > end` is false, so execution proceeds to `len = *p++`, which dereferences `p` at the `end` boundary. Since `end` typically points one past the last valid byte, this is a one-byte out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with p pointing to end
Check p > end fails since p == end
len = *p++ dereferences p at the end boundary, reading one byte out of bounds

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is specifically about a zero-length buffer being mishandled (len == 0), not about an OOB read of the length byte when p == end. The fix adds `len == 0` to the condition, which rejects zero-length buffers. The finding describes a hypothetical different boundary issue (p == end causing OOB read of the length byte itself), which is not what this CVE is about. The actual vulnerability is that when len is legitimately read as 0, the function returns a zero-length allocation that causes the auth service to crash downstream.

actual_issue_summary: In Dovecot's RPA authentication mechanism, the rpa_read_buffer function did not check for a zero-length value read from the input. When len == 0, the function would return 0-length buffers that would be mishandled by callers, crashing the auth service. The fix adds a `len == 0` check to reject zero-length buffers early.

cve_desc: tinyexr 0.9.5 has a integer overflow over-write in tinyexr::DecodePixelData in tinyexr.h, related to OpenEXR code.

Adding num_scanline_blocks (up to 32) to it causes signed integer overflow, which is undefined behavior in C/C++. This could lead to unexpected control flow, bypassing the num_lines <= 0 check, and causing out-of-bounds memory access in DecodePixelData.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the integer overflow issue with line_no from untrusted data. The CVE is about 'integer overflow over-write in DecodePixelData' and the fix adds a bounds check on line_no (rejecting values > 2<<20). The finding identifies the same root cause - an unchecked large line_no leading to integer overflow and subsequent out-of-bounds write.

Without bounds checking, this large value propagates into DecodePixelData causing an integer overflow and out-of-bounds memory write. The fix adds a check rejecting line_no values with absolute value greater than 2<<20 (approximately 1 million).

cve_desc: scheme/webauthn.c in Glewlwyd SSO server through 2.5.3 has a buffer overflow during FIDO2 signature validation in webauthn registration.

finding.description: Stack buffer overflow in `data_signed`. The fixed-size 200-byte stack buffer receives multiple variable-length inputs (rpid_hash, client_data_hash, credential_id, cert_x, cert_y) via memcpy without any bounds checking. The total size is 1 + rpid_hash_len + 32 + credential_id_len + 1 + cert_x_len + cert_y_len. Since credential_id can be up to 1023 bytes per the WebAuthn specification, and these lengths are caller-controlled, the buffer can be easily overflowed.

step_by_step_execution.description: A 200-byte stack buffer `data_signed[200]` is declared.
data_signed[0] is set to 0x0, offset becomes 1.
rpid_hash (rpid_hash_len bytes) is copied into data_signed.
client_data_hash (32 bytes) is copied into data_signed.
credential_id (credential_id_len bytes, attacker-controlled, up to 1023 bytes) is copied into data_signed. If credential_id_len is large enough, this overflows the 200-byte buffer.
Additional data (cert_x, cert_y) is also copied, further exceeding the buffer.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-40818: a buffer overflow in the fixed-size stack buffer `data_signed[200]` during FIDO2 signature validation. The fix replaces the stack buffer with a dynamically allocated buffer sized to the actual data. The CWE-787 (out-of-bounds write) matches the CVE's CWE.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a stack buffer overflow in `check_attestation_fido_u2f` where `data_signed` is a fixed 200-byte stack buffer that receives variable-length data (rpid_hash, client_data_hash, credential_id, cert_x, cert_y) without bounds checking. The fix changes `data_signed` from a stack array to a dynamically allocated buffer sized to fit the actual data.

cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a null pointer dereference vulnerability in the mpgviddmx_process function in reframe_mpgvid.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service. This vulnerability is possibly due to an incomplete fix for CVE-2021-40566.

finding.description: Integer overflow in `ctx->hdr_store_size + pck_size` (both u32) can wrap around to a small value, causing the reallocation check to be skipped. The subsequent memcpy then writes `pck_size` bytes at offset `ctx->hdr_store_size` in a buffer that is too small, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

memcpy writes past buffer

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about a null pointer dereference caused by insufficient bytes remaining (remain < 5) before parsing headers. The fix adds a bounds check for remain < 5 before the header parsing section. This finding describes an integer overflow in a completely different part of the code and is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

finding.description: Integer underflow of u64 variable `size` when gf_m4v_parse_frame returns GF_EOS with size < 3. The assert(size >= 3) is compiled out in release builds, so size -= 3 underflows to a very large u64 value, causing heap buffer overflow in subsequent memcpy.

size -= 3 underflows
memcpy uses underflowed size

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is a null pointer dereference caused by missing a check for remain < 5 before header parsing. The fix adds this bounds check. This finding describes a potential integer underflow in a different code path and is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: After dispatching remaining bytes in the main loop (the `if (current>0)` block), `remain` could become less than 5. Without the added check, the code proceeds to parse start code headers by calling gf_bs_reassign_buffer and gf_bs_read_int with insufficient data in the buffer, leading to a null pointer dereference. The fix adds a `remain < 5` check before the header parsing section to store the remaining bytes and break out of the loop.

cve_desc: In ImageMagick before 6.9.9-0 and 7.x before 7.0.6-1, the ReadOneMNGImage function in coders/png.c has an out-of-bounds read with the MNG CLIP chunk.

These values are used as indices into `mng_info->exists[]`, `mng_info->frozen[]`, and `mng_info->object_clip[]` arrays without any bounds checking against MNG_MAX_OBJECTS. This leads to an out-of-bounds read/write when the object indices exceed the array sizes.

step_by_step_execution.description: MNG CLIP chunk is read with length > 3
first_object and last_object are parsed as 16-bit values from chunk data
Loop iterates from first_object to last_object without bounds check against MNG_MAX_OBJECTS
mng_info->exists[i] is accessed out of bounds, causing out-of-bounds read
If the OOB read returns truthy, mng_info->object_clip[i] is written out of bounds

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches the CVE-2017-13139 fix. The diff adds bounds checking `(i < 0) || (i >= MNG_MAX_OBJECTS)` in the CLIP chunk handler loop, which is precisely what this finding identifies as missing.

finding.description: In the mng_DISC chunk handler, object indices are read as 16-bit values from user-controlled data and passed directly to MngInfoDiscardObject without bounds validation. The index can be 0-65535, exceeding MNG_MAX_OBJECTS array bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: MNG DISC chunk is read with non-zero length
Object index i is parsed as 16-bit value from chunk data
MngInfoDiscardObject is called with potentially out-of-range index

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential issue in the DISC chunk handler, but the CVE-2017-13139 and its fix specifically address the CLIP chunk handler's missing bounds check. The DISC handler issue is a different code path not addressed by this CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The MNG CLIP chunk handler in ReadOneMNGImage iterates over object IDs from first_object to last_object (16-bit user-controlled values) without checking if they are within the valid range [0, MNG_MAX_OBJECTS). This causes out-of-bounds reads on mng_info->exists[], mng_info->frozen[], and mng_info->object_clip[] arrays. The fix adds a bounds check `(i < 0) || (i >= MNG_MAX_OBJECTS)` to skip invalid object IDs.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.description: The local pointer `dequeue_node` is declared without initialization. If the loop over `train_nodes` never finds a dequeue op (i.e., `IsDequeueOp` is never true), `dequeue_node` remains uninitialized. The subsequent `if (dequeue_node)` check reads this uninitialized pointer, which is undefined behavior. Depending on the garbage value, the branch may be taken, leading to dereferencing an invalid pointer (`dequeue_node->name()`).

The loop iterates over train_nodes but none satisfy IsDequeueOp, so dequeue_node is never assigned.

The condition `if (dequeue_node)` reads the uninitialized pointer, which is undefined behavior. If the garbage value is non-null, the branch is taken and `dequeue_node->name()` dereferences an invalid pointer.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-41225: the uninitialized `dequeue_node` pointer, which the fix initializes to nullptr.

actual_issue_summary: The pointer `dequeue_node` was declared without initialization (`const NodeDef* dequeue_node;`). If no Dequeue node exists in `train_nodes`, the pointer remains uninitialized and is subsequently read in an `if` check and potentially dereferenced, causing undefined behavior. The fix initializes it to `nullptr`.

cve_desc: An unauthenticated stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in common.c's handle_PORT in uftpd FTP server versions 2.10 and earlier can be abused to cause a crash and could potentially lead to remote code execution.

A malicious client can supply large integer values (e.g., very large negative numbers like -2147483648) that produce a string far exceeding 15 characters, causing a stack buffer overflow.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. A patch is available in commit 450baca which should be included in the next release. There are no known workarounds for this issue.

A specially crafted STUN message with many attributes can overflow this array, causing a stack buffer overflow (CWE-787). The fix adds a bounds check (msg->attr_count < attr_max_cnt) to the loop condition.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.description: Division by zero occurs when both `input_requested_min_float` and `input_requested_max_float` are 0.0f. The variable `requested_min_max` is computed as `std::max(std::abs(0.0f), std::abs(0.0f))` which equals 0.0f, and is then used as a divisor in the scales computation loop.

step_by_step_execution.description: Both input_requested_min and input_requested_max are 0.0f
requested_min_max computed as max(abs(0.0f), abs(0.0f)) = 0.0f
Division by requested_min_max causes division by zero

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about incomplete validation of input tensor dimensions and scalar inputs, leading to null pointer dereference or out-of-bounds heap access. The fix moves the `input.dims() == 4` check before data access, adds checks that requested min/max are scalars, and converts DCHECK to OP_REQUIRES. The division by zero issue described in this finding is not what the CVE addresses - the fix does not add any check for `requested_min_max != 0`.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerable code accesses tensor data (e.g., calling .flat<float>()(0) on input_requested_min/max) before validating that the tensors have the expected shapes (scalar, 4D, matching depths). This allows null pointer dereference or out-of-bounds reads. The fix reorders checks to validate dimensions before accessing data, adds scalar checks for requested min/max inputs, and converts a debug-only DCHECK to a proper OP_REQUIRES validation.

cve_desc: Integer overflow in the VNC display driver in QEMU before 2.1.0 allows attachers to cause a denial of service (process crash) via a CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message, which triggers an infinite loop.

finding.description: Integer overflow in VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT handling. The expression `8 + dlen` (where dlen is uint32_t) can overflow, causing the function to return a small value. The caller then re-invokes the function with a buffer much smaller than expected, leading to an out-of-bounds read when `client_cut_text` is called with the original large dlen but a tiny data buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A malicious VNC client sends a CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message.
First call with len==1, function returns 8 to request more data.
Second call with len==8, dlen is read as a very large value near UINT32_MAX.
The expression 8 + dlen overflows uint32_t, producing a small value (7), which is returned.
The function is re-invoked but the requested size and actual buffer are inconsistent, leading to potential infinite loop or out-of-bounds access.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the integer overflow in `8 + dlen` in the CLIENT_CUT_TEXT handler, which is exactly the vulnerability described in CVE-2015-5239. When dlen is near UINT32_MAX, the addition overflows, causing the return value to be small and triggering an infinite loop (as the CVE states) or other misbehavior. The fix adds a check limiting dlen to 1MB, preventing the overflow. The finding's CWE-190 (integer overflow) matches the root cause, even though the stated consequence (OOB read) differs slightly from the CVE's stated consequence (infinite loop).

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message handling: when dlen is very large (e.g., 0xFFFFFFF8 or higher), `8 + dlen` overflows, causing the function to return a small value. This leads to an infinite loop as the protocol handler repeatedly requests insufficient data. The fix adds a 1MB limit on the payload size.

When `needle + 4` overflows (needle near UT64_MAX), the comparison `needle + 4 < to` could behave incorrectly, and expressions like `buf + needle - from` could compute wrong offsets due to operator precedence, leading to out-of-bounds reads from the buffer. The fix adds an overflow guard (`needle < UT64_MAX - 4`), rewrites the comparison to avoid overflow (`needle < to - 4`), and adds parentheses to ensure correct subtraction order (`buf + (needle - from)`).

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.FractionalAvgPoolGrad` can be tricked into accessing data outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/fractional_avg_pool_op.cc#L205) does not validate that the input tensor is non-empty. Thus, code constructs an empty `EigenDoubleMatrixMap` and then accesses this buffer with indices that are outside of the empty area. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 0f931751fb20f565c4e94aa6df58d54a003cdb30. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

finding.description: The code does not validate that out_depth == in_depth or that out_batch == in_batch. The in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat is mapped with in_depth rows, but the inner loop iterates d from 0 to out_depth. If an attacker provides an out_backprop tensor with out_depth > in_depth, this causes an out-of-bounds read/write on in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat. Similarly, iterating b up to out_batch with in_index computed using in_rows can overflow the column dimension if out_batch > in_batch.

step_by_step_execution.description: out_depth and in_depth are read from different tensors
Loop iterates d from 0 to out_depth accessing in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat which has in_depth rows

The fix adds checks that these values are != 0. The finding describes a different issue — a mismatch between out_depth/in_depth and out_batch/in_batch — which is not what this CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: The FractionalAvgPoolGrad implementation does not validate that the original input tensor shape dimensions (in_batch, in_rows, in_cols, in_depth) are non-zero. When any dimension is 0, an empty EigenDoubleMatrixMap is created and subsequently accessed with out-of-bounds indices, leading to heap buffer access violations. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks that all four dimensions are non-zero.

cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11. This vulnerability is capable of arbitrary code execution.

finding.description: In the delete branch (mod == 'd', dir == '<'), `pos` is decremented before any check that it remains non-negative. If `pos` is 0 when entering this branch, it becomes -1. The subsequent bounds check (`l < 0 || d + l > decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX`) does not catch this because `l = *decoded_size - (-1) = *decoded_size + 1` is positive, and `d + l = decoded + *decoded_size` is likely within the buffer limit. This leads to an out-of-bounds read at `decoded[pos]` (i.e., `decoded[-1]`) and an out-of-bounds write via `memmove` to `decoded + pos` (i.e., one byte before the buffer).

step_by_step_execution.description: Function starts with pos = *decoded_size and dir = '<', mod = 'i'.
A rule byte c <= 4 sets mod to 'd' (delete) and dir to '<'.
A rule byte c > 10 && c < 20 adjusts pos downward. With appropriate values, pos becomes 0.
A rule byte c > 20 enters the else branch. Since mod == 'd', the delete path is taken. Since dir == '<', pos is decremented: pos--.
Bounds check evaluates: l = *decoded_size - (-1) = *decoded_size + 1 (positive), d + l = decoded + (-1) + (*decoded_size + 1) = decoded + *decoded_size (within buffer). Check passes.
decoded[pos] is accessed, reading decoded[-1] — an out-of-bounds read. If c matches, memmove writes to decoded + (-1), an out-of-bounds write.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the missing `pos < 0` check in the delete branch, which is exactly what the fix addresses. The fix adds `pos < 0` checks in both the insert and delete branches to prevent negative index access. This matches the CVE description of buffer over-read in the inflections parser with corrupt input.

actual_issue_summary: The inflections parser had wrong boundary checks: (1) the `pos < 0` check after position adjustment (c > 10 && c < 20) was in the wrong place and was removed, (2) neither the insert nor delete branches checked for `pos < 0`, allowing negative indices to be used for buffer access. The fix adds `pos < 0` checks in both the insert and delete operation branches, and also fixes the delete branch to check `s + l` instead of `d + l` for the upper bound.

cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

The fix adds a check `instr[y].size >= 4` before entering that code path.
