Without this check, `ts_fidx` could be decremented below zero (when it's already 0), causing an out-of-bounds read. The finding describes a completely different issue (buffer overflow in STATE_REP via STRMOVE) that is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: In STATE_PLAIN's multi-byte character handling, when `sp->ts_isdiff == DIFF_INSERT`, the code unconditionally decrements `sp->ts_fidx` without checking if it's already 0. This causes `ts_fidx` to become negative (or wrap around as unsigned), leading to an out-of-bounds read when `fword[sp->ts_fidx]` is subsequently accessed. The fix adds a `sp->ts_fidx > 0` guard before the decrement.

cve_desc: The ip6gre_err function in net/ipv6/ip6_gre.c in the Linux kernel allows remote attackers to have unspecified impact via vectors involving GRE flags in an IPv6 packet, which trigger an out-of-bounds access.

finding.description: Out-of-bounds read when computing the GRE key for tunnel lookup. `grehlen` includes `offset` (the distance from skb->data to the GRE header), but it is used as if it only represents the GRE header length. The expression `((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1` indexes relative to `p` (which already points to `skb->data + offset`), so the final read address is `skb->data + offset + 4*(grehlen/4) - 4 = skb->data + 2*offset + <GRE_hdr_fields> - 4`. For a typical offset of 40 (IPv6 header), this reads ~40 bytes past the intended key position, well beyond the `grehlen` bytes guaranteed by `pskb_may_pull`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an IPv6 GRE error packet. `offset` is the offset from skb->data to the GRE header (typically 40 for IPv6 header).
`p` is set to point to skb->data + offset (the GRE header). `grehlen` is initialized to `offset + 4` = 44.
GRE flags are read from p[0]. If GRE_KEY flag is set and GRE_CSUM is not set, grehlen becomes 44 + 4 = 48.
pskb_may_pull ensures at least 48 bytes are available from skb->data.

The key extraction expression `((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1` computes the index as (48/4) - 1 = 11. This reads at p + 11*4 = p + 44 = skb->data + 40 + 44 = skb->data + 84. The actual GRE key should be at skb->data + 40 + 4 = skb->data + 44. This reads 40 bytes past the intended location, causing an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: skb->data + 84 (40 bytes past intended key at skb->data + 44)

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely identifies the CVE-2017-5897 vulnerability: grehlen includes the IPv6 header offset, so when used to index relative to p (which already accounts for offset), it causes an out-of-bounds read ~40 bytes past the intended GRE key position. This matches the commit message and the CWE-125 classification.

actual_issue_summary: In ip6gre_err(), grehlen was initialized to `offset + 4` (including the IPv6 header size), but was used in the key extraction expression `((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1` relative to `p` which already points to `skb->data + offset`. This double-counting of the offset caused an out-of-bounds read ~40 bytes past the intended GRE key field. The fix separates grehlen to only track GRE header length and uses a dedicated key_off variable to track the key's position.

cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

finding.description: The memcpy copies sizeof(struct capability) bytes from pnt+1 into cap, but at that point only 3 bytes from pnt (action, capability code, capability length) have been validated to exist in the buffer. The struct capability likely contains fields beyond code and length (e.g., the mpc sub-structure with afi/safi), making sizeof(struct capability) larger than 2. This means the memcpy can read past the end of the input buffer. The bounds check against cap.length happens only after the memcpy has already performed the over-read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a pointer pnt and length, computes end = pnt + length.
The loop checks pnt + 3 > end, ensuring at least 3 bytes remain (action, code, length fields).

state.value: <= end, so at least 3 bytes available

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy(&cap, pnt + 1, sizeof(struct capability)) copies sizeof(struct capability) bytes from pnt+1, which may exceed the available buffer if sizeof(struct capability) > (end - pnt - 1).
This results in an out-of-bounds read from the input buffer before the cap.length validation check is performed.

state.value: contains data read beyond buffer boundary

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the CVE-2022-37032 vulnerability: the memcpy of sizeof(struct capability) bytes occurs before validating cap.length, causing an out-of-bounds read. The fix moves the memcpy after the length check and only copies the smaller capability_mp_data structure.

actual_issue_summary: In bgp_capability_msg_parse, a memcpy of sizeof(struct capability) bytes was performed before validating the capability length field, allowing an out-of-bounds read past the input buffer. The fix reorders operations to validate lengths before copying data, and copies only the needed capability_mp_data structure instead of the full capability struct. Additionally, afi/safi validation was added via bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices.

cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from PYC(python) files. A user opening a malicious PYC file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 68948017423a12786704e54227b8b2f918c2fd27 contains a patch.

finding.description: When n1 is obtained from get_st32 and equals 0xFFFFFFFF (i.e., -1 as a signed int cast to ut32), the expression `n1 + 1` wraps around to 0 due to unsigned integer overflow. malloc(0) returns a small or zero-size allocation, but rz_buf_read then attempts to write up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into that allocation, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: get_st32 reads a signed 32-bit value from the PYC file, returning -1 (0xFFFFFFFF as ut32) for n1
n1 + 1 overflows to 0, malloc(0) returns a tiny or zero-size buffer
rz_buf_read attempts to write n1 (0xFFFFFFFF) bytes into s1, causing heap buffer overflow

judgments.reasoning: This exactly matches the CVE: an integer overflow in n1+1 leads to an undersized malloc and subsequent out-of-bounds write. The fix adds UT32_ADD_OVFCHK(n1, 1) to prevent this.

finding.description: Same integer overflow vulnerability as with n1, but for n2. If n2 is 0xFFFFFFFF, malloc(0) is called and rz_buf_read overflows the resulting allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: get_st32 reads a signed 32-bit value from the PYC file, returning -1 (0xFFFFFFFF as ut32) for n2
n2 + 1 overflows to 0, malloc(0) returns a tiny or zero-size buffer
rz_buf_read attempts to write n2 (0xFFFFFFFF) bytes into s2, causing heap buffer overflow

judgments.reasoning: This is the same class of vulnerability for n2, also fixed by adding UT32_ADD_OVFCHK(n2, 1). It's a second instance of the same CVE issue in the same function.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in get_complex_object when n1 or n2 is 0xFFFFFFFF causes n+1 to wrap to 0, leading to a zero-size malloc followed by a large out-of-bounds write via rz_buf_read. The fix adds UT32_ADD_OVFCHK checks before the malloc calls.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in Poppler through 0.78.0. There is a divide-by-zero error in the function SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill at SplashOutputDev.cc.

finding.description: Division by zero when both ctm[0] and ctm[1] are zero (or both matc[0] and matc[1] are zero). The condition `splashAbs(ctm[1]) > splashAbs(ctm[0])` is false when both are zero, leading to the else branch which divides by ctm[0] (which is zero).

step_by_step_execution.description: ctm[0] and ctm[1] are both zero
The condition splashAbs(ctm[1]) > splashAbs(ctm[0]) is false, so the else branch is taken
Division by ctm[0] which is zero occurs

judgments.reasoning: The finding identifies a divide-by-zero at a different location than the actual CVE. The CVE fix adds a check for surface_width == 0 or surface_height == 0 before the integer divisions `repeatX = result_width / surface_width` and `repeatY = result_height / surface_height`. The finding points to floating-point division by ctm[0]/matc[0], which is a different code path and not what was fixed in the commit.

actual_issue_summary: Divide-by-zero occurs when surface_width or surface_height is 0 in the else branch, at the lines `repeatX = result_width / surface_width` and `repeatY = result_height / surface_height`. The fix adds an early return check for surface_width == 0 || surface_height == 0 before these integer divisions.

cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

finding.description: When groupCode==42 is processed before any groupCode==10 has been seen, vertexIndex is still -1 (its initial value set when groupCode==90 is handled). The check `vertexIndex<maxVertices` passes because -1 < maxVertices, but unlike the groupCode<=30 branch, there is no `vertexIndex>=0` check. This leads to an out-of-bounds write at `vertices[4*(-1) + 3]` = `vertices[-1]`.

step_by_step_execution.description: groupCode==90 is processed, allocating the vertices array and setting vertexIndex=-1
groupCode==42 is encountered before any groupCode==10, so vertexIndex remains -1
The condition `groupCode==42 && vertexIndex<maxVertices` is true since -1 < maxVertices
Out-of-bounds write at vertices[4*(-1)+3] = vertices[-1]

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely identifies the missing vertexIndex>=0 check in the groupCode==42 branch, which is exactly what the fix adds. This allows a heap buffer overflow (writing before the allocated array) when vertexIndex is -1, matching CVE-2021-21897.

actual_issue_summary: When groupCode==42 is processed while vertexIndex is -1 (before any groupCode==10 increments it), the missing vertexIndex>=0 check allows an out-of-bounds heap write at a negative index. The fix adds vertexIndex>=0 checks to both the groupCode<=30 and groupCode==42 branches.

cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-40 Q16, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the function WritePNMImage of coders/pnm.c, which allows an attacker to cause a denial of service or possibly information disclosure via a crafted image file. This is related to SetGrayscaleImage in MagickCore/quantize.c.

finding.description: When the image has storage_class == PseudoClass, colormap_index is allocated with only (image->colors + 1) elements. However, in the final pixel-processing loop, the array is indexed by ScaleQuantumToMap(GetPixelIndex(image, q)). ScaleQuantumToMap can scale a small index value (0 to image->colors-1) into a much larger range (up to MaxMap, which can be 65535), causing an out-of-bounds read on the heap buffer colormap_index.

step_by_step_execution.description: Image with storage_class == PseudoClass enters SetGrayscaleImage, and colormap_index is allocated with (image->colors + 1) elements, which can be small.
In the final loop, for each pixel, ScaleQuantumToMap(GetPixelIndex(image, q)) is used as an index into colormap_index. ScaleQuantumToMap can return values up to MaxMap (65535).
If the scaled index exceeds image->colors, the access colormap_index[index] is an out-of-bounds heap read.

state.value: out-of-bounds, up to 65535 vs allocated size of image->colors+1

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the CVE-2019-11598 vulnerability: the colormap_index buffer is too small (allocated based on image->colors+1) but indexed by ScaleQuantumToMap which can return values up to MaxMap. The fix ensures the buffer is at least MaxMap in size using MagickMax, which matches exactly.

actual_issue_summary: The colormap_index buffer in SetGrayscaleImage was allocated with size based on image->colors+1 (PseudoClass) or MaxColormapSize+1, but later indexed by ScaleQuantumToMap() which can return values up to MaxMap (65535). When the buffer was smaller than MaxMap, this caused a heap-based buffer over-read. The fix ensures the buffer is always at least MaxMap elements by using MagickMax.

cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

finding.description: When Bitmap_File_Head.biSize is 108 or 124 (BMP Version 4 or 5), the code reads biSize-4 bytes (104 or 120 bytes) into a stack buffer of only 64 bytes, causing a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted BMP file is opened with biSize set to 108 or 124.
The code enters the branch for BMP Version 4 or 5 and calls ReadOK to read biSize-4 bytes into buffer.
ReadOK writes 104 or 120 bytes into the 64-byte stack buffer, overflowing it.

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely identifies the vulnerability fixed by the commit: the 64-byte stack buffer is too small for BMP v4/v5 headers (108-4=104 or 124-4=120 bytes). The fix increases the buffer to 128 bytes. This matches CVE-2022-32323 which describes a heap overflow in input-bmp.c (though it's technically a stack overflow, the root cause is the same undersized buffer).

actual_issue_summary: The stack buffer 'buffer[64]' in input_bmp_reader was too small to hold BMP Version 4 (108 bytes) or Version 5 (124 bytes) headers. When biSize is 108 or 124, reading biSize-4 bytes (104 or 120) into a 64-byte buffer causes a buffer overflow. The fix increases the buffer size from 64 to 128 bytes.

cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

finding.description: The loop advances `addr` by `pervq_inflight_size` on each iteration without verifying that the resulting pointer stays within the mmap'd region of size `mmap_size`. If `num_queues * pervq_inflight_size + mmap_offset > mmap_size`, later iterations assign out-of-bounds addresses to virtqueue inflight pointers, which will cause out-of-bounds reads/writes when those pointers are later dereferenced.

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop advances addr

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-0669) is about file descriptor leaks: a malicious vhost-user master can attach unexpected fds to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD messages, which are never closed by the slave, leading to fd exhaustion and denial of service. The fix adds `validate_msg_fds(dev, ctx, 0)` to reject messages with unexpected fds. This finding describes a hypothetical out-of-bounds memory write in the loop, which is an entirely different issue unrelated to the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: A malicious vhost-user master could attach unexpected file descriptors as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD messages. The function did not validate or close these unexpected fds, leading to fd exhaustion (denial of service). The fix adds a call to `validate_msg_fds(dev, ctx, 0)` to ensure no unexpected fds are attached.

cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

finding.description: The function is recursive with no depth limit. A carefully crafted image (e.g., a serpentine pattern) can cause recursion depth proportional to the number of pixels in the image, leading to stack exhaustion and a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: gdImageFillToBorder is called with attacker-controlled x, y values and a crafted image
Without bounds checking on x and y against image dimensions, out-of-bounds coordinates can be used, reading garbage pixel data that never matches the border color
The function recursively calls itself for lines above and below, with no recursion depth limit, leading to deep stack usage

state.value: unbounded, proportional to image size or garbage data patterns

step_by_step_execution.description: Stack is exhausted, causing a crash (denial of service)

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue behind CVE-2015-8874: unbounded recursion in gdImageFillToBorder leading to stack exhaustion. The CVE description explicitly states 'stack consumption vulnerability.' The fix adds bounds checking on x and y coordinates which prevents out-of-bounds reads that could cause patterns leading to excessive recursion. CWE-674 (Uncontrolled Recursion) is a reasonable classification closely related to the NVD's CWE-119 (which describes the stack consumption consequence).

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerable function lacks bounds checking on the x and y parameters against image dimensions (im->sx, im->sy). When x >= im->sx or y >= im->sy, the function can read out-of-bounds pixel data that may never match the border color, causing unbounded recursion and stack exhaustion (crash/DoS). The fix adds clamping of x and y to valid image boundaries.

cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

finding.description: Heap buffer overflow via sprintf. The string "/tmp/sd000000" is 13 characters + null terminator = 14 bytes allocated by omStrDup. sprintf writes the PID at offset 7. On 64-bit Linux, PIDs can reach 4194304 (7 digits). Writing 7 digits + null at offset 7 requires 15 bytes total, overflowing the 14-byte heap buffer by 1 byte.

step_by_step_execution.description: A 14-byte heap buffer is allocated containing "/tmp/sd000000\0"
sprintf writes PID digits at offset 7, potentially overflowing if PID >= 1000000

state.value: 7 digits + null = 8 bytes, total needed = 15 bytes

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about a predictable /tmp pathname enabling symlink attacks and privilege escalation, not about a potential heap buffer overflow from large PIDs. The fix replaces the predictable filename with mkstemp() for secure temporary file creation. The buffer overflow is a theoretically possible secondary issue but is not what CVE-2022-40299 describes.

finding.description: Symlink race condition (TOCTOU) due to predictable temporary file name in /tmp. The filename /tmp/sd<PID> is entirely predictable. An attacker can create a symlink at that path before the program opens it, causing the program to write to or read from an arbitrary file.

step_by_step_execution.description: A predictable filename is constructed from /tmp/sd + PID
An attacker creates a symlink at /tmp/sd<PID> pointing to a target file before fopen is called
fopen opens the symlink target for writing, overwriting the attacker-chosen file with Singular user privileges

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly matches CVE-2022-40299: a predictable /tmp pathname in sdb.cc allows symlink attacks by local users. The fix replaces the predictable name with mkstemp() to prevent exactly this issue.

actual_issue_summary: The sdb_edit function in sdb.cc used a predictable temporary file path (/tmp/sd<PID>) constructed from the process ID. This allowed local attackers to exploit symlink race conditions to overwrite arbitrary files or potentially execute code. The fix replaced the predictable path and fopen with mkstemp() which creates a unique, unpredictable temporary file securely.

cve_desc: A memory leak in the cx23888_ir_probe() function in drivers/media/pci/cx23885/cx23888-ir.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering kfifo_alloc() failures, aka CID-a7b2df76b42b.

finding.description: When kfifo_alloc fails, the function returns -ENOMEM without freeing the previously allocated 'state' object, leading to a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: state is allocated via kzalloc
kfifo_alloc fails, returning non-zero
Function returns -ENOMEM without calling kfree(state)

state.value: leaked — no reference retained, never freed

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2019-19054: when kfifo_alloc fails, the previously allocated 'state' is not freed, causing a memory leak. The fix adds kfree(state) before the return. CWE-401 matches as well.

actual_issue_summary: When kfifo_alloc() fails in cx23888_ir_probe(), the function returns -ENOMEM without freeing the previously kzalloc'd 'state' struct, causing a memory leak. The fix adds kfree(state) before the error return.

cve_desc: A null pointer de-reference was found in the way samba kerberos server handled missing sname in TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Server - Request). An authenticated user could use this flaw to crash the samba server.

finding.description: The return value of krb5_make_principal() is not checked in the capath referral loop. If the call fails (e.g., due to memory allocation failure), sp remains NULL (it was set to NULL just before the call). The subsequent call to krb5_unparse_name(context, sp, ...) will dereference the NULL pointer, causing a crash (denial of service).

step_by_step_execution.description: krb5_make_principal fails due to memory allocation
krb5_unparse_name dereferences NULL sp

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is specifically about a missing NULL check on `b->sname` (the `s` variable) when it is not set in the TGS-REQ and the enc_tkt_in_skey path is not taken. The fix adds `else if (s == NULL)` check. These findings describe a different issue: unchecked return values of krb5_make_principal() in the capath/referral code paths, which is unrelated to the missing sname validation.

finding.description: In the need_referral path, the return value of krb5_make_principal() is also not checked. If it fails, sp remains NULL and the subsequent krb5_unparse_name dereferences a NULL pointer, causing a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: krb5_make_principal fails in need_referral path
krb5_unparse_name dereferences NULL sp

judgments.reasoning: Same as Finding 0 - this describes an unchecked return value of krb5_make_principal() in the need_referral path, not the missing sname validation that CVE-2021-3671 is about.

actual_issue_summary: When b->sname is NULL (missing sname in TGS-REQ) and the enc_tkt_in_skey option is not set, the variable `s` remains NULL. It is then dereferenced as `*s` in the call to `_krb5_principalname2krb5_principal(context, &sp, *s, r)`, causing a NULL pointer dereference crash. The fix adds an `else if (s == NULL)` check to return an error before the dereference.

cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

finding.description: The pipePath string (from user-controlled endpoint.name()) is copied into local.sun_path using strcpy without any length check. The sun_path field in sockaddr_un is a fixed-size buffer (typically 108 bytes on Linux). If pipePath exceeds this size, a stack buffer overflow occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: pipePath is derived from endpoint.name() which could be longer than sun_path buffer
strcpy copies the entire string into the fixed-size buffer without bounds checking

judgments.reasoning: CVE-2022-24950 is specifically about a race condition (CWE-362) in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId() that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization sockets. The fix adds a recursive_mutex to protect concurrent access to idInfoMap. While the strcpy→strncpy change was part of the same commit, it is a separate fix unrelated to the CVE. The finding describes a buffer overflow, not the race condition that constitutes CVE-2022-24950.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2022-24950 is a race condition in UserTerminalRouter where acceptNewConnection() and getInfoForId() access a shared idInfoMap without synchronization. The fix adds a recursive_mutex (routerMutex) with lock_guards in both methods. The strcpy→strncpy change in PipeSocketHandler was a separate hardening fix in the same commit.

cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-13 Q16, there is an infinite loop in the ReadBMPImage function of the coders/bmp.c file. Remote attackers could leverage this vulnerability to cause a denial of service via a crafted bmp file.

finding.description: For 1-bit BMP images, the pixel index (0 or 1) is written directly without validating it against the colormap size (via IsValidColormapIndex, which IS used in the 4-bit and 8-bit cases). A crafted BMP with bits_per_pixel=1 and number_colors=1 will allocate a colormap with only 1 entry (index 0). When pixel data contains set bits, index 1 is stored, and the subsequent SyncImage call accesses colormap[1], which is an out-of-bounds read. The validation check only rejects number_colors > (1 << bits_per_pixel), so number_colors=1 passes the check (1 > 2 is false).

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted BMP with bits_per_pixel=1 and number_colors=1 is processed

judgments.reasoning: The CVE-2018-18024 is about an infinite loop (CWE-835) caused by start_position not being updated on subsequent loop iterations when ba_offset was non-zero. The fix unconditionally sets start_position=TellBlob(image)-2 each iteration. The finding describes a completely different issue (out-of-bounds colormap read for 1-bit BMPs), which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: The fix removes the conditional `if (bmp_info.ba_offset == 0)` guard on updating `start_position`, so it is always updated to `TellBlob(image)-2` at each loop iteration. Without this fix, when processing a multi-image BMP with ba_offset != 0, start_position would retain its initial value, causing SeekBlob calls using start_position to seek back to the same location repeatedly, resulting in an infinite loop (CWE-835).

cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE fix changes `hdr->mappingCount` to `cache->n_maps` in the va2pa() call. The vulnerability was that `hdr->mappingCount` could be a crafted/incorrect value causing a heap-buffer-overflow when va2pa reads beyond the maps array. The finding identifies a different potential OOB read in the depArray loop, which is not what this CVE addresses.

A crafted dyld cache file could have a `hdr->mappingCount` larger than the actual number of maps, causing va2pa to read beyond the allocated maps buffer, resulting in a heap-buffer-overflow.

cve_desc: There's a flaw in jasper's jpc encoder in versions prior to 2.0.23. Crafted input provided to jasper by an attacker could cause an arbitrary out-of-bounds write. This could potentially affect data confidentiality, integrity, or application availability.

finding.description: The user-controlled `maxrlvls` value from option parsing is used directly as a loop bound to write into fixed-size arrays `tccp->prcwidthexpns` and `tccp->prcheightexpns` before any bounds validation occurs. The validation check (`if (cp->tccp.maxrlvls < 1)`) happens much later in the function, after the out-of-bounds write has already occurred.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides crafted input with a large maxrlvls option value
The OPT_MAXRLVLS case sets tccp->maxrlvls to the attacker-controlled value without bounds checking
The loop iterates using tccp->maxrlvls as the bound, writing past the end of the fixed-size arrays

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely matches CVE-2020-27828. The fix adds a bounds check on maxrlvls immediately after parsing the option (comparing against JPC_MAXRLVLS), preventing the subsequent out-of-bounds writes to the prcwidthexpns and prcheightexpns arrays.

The fix adds bounds checking immediately after parsing the maxrlvls option.

cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

finding.description: Out-of-bounds read on tok->args[2]. When tok->n_args is exactly 2 (which satisfies the `tok->n_args > 1` check), accessing tok->args[2] reads past the end of the args array. The error message incorrectly uses index 2 instead of index 1.

step_by_step_execution.description: Token K_NETWORK_STATUS_VERSION is found with tok->n_args == 2
The condition tok->n_args > 1 is true, so the block is entered
networkstatus_parse_flavor_name returns -1 for an unknown flavor
The error log accesses tok->args[2] which is out of bounds since args only has indices 0 and 1

judgments.reasoning: CVE-2012-3517 is about a use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c related to failed DNS requests. The finding correctly identifies the bug in the provided diff (out-of-bounds array access in networkstatus parsing), but this commit appears to be unrelated to CVE-2012-3517. The finding describes a different vulnerability (CWE-125 out-of-bounds read) than the CVE (CWE-399/use-after-free in dns.c).

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2012-3517 is a use-after-free in dns.c related to failed DNS requests in Tor before 0.2.2.38. The provided commit actually fixes a different bug (ticket #6530): an out-of-bounds array access where tok->args[2] is used instead of tok->args[1] when logging an error about an unknown flavor in networkstatus vote parsing, which could cause a segfault.

cve_desc: The GNU C Library (aka glibc or libc6) before 2.27 contains an off-by-one error leading to a heap-based buffer overflow in the glob function in glob.c, related to the processing of home directories using the ~ operator followed by a long string.

finding.description: Off-by-one heap buffer overflow when extracting a username from a tilde expression like "~username/path". The buffer is allocated as `end_name - dirname` bytes, but `mempcpy` copies `end_name - dirname` bytes starting from `dirname + 1` (filling the buffer completely), and then a null terminator is written one byte past the end of the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A pattern like "~username/path" is passed to glob with GLOB_TILDE flag. dirname is set to "~username" and filename to "path". The code enters the else branch for ~NAME processing.
end_name is found by strchr(dirname, '/'), pointing to the '/' character.
Buffer newp is allocated with size end_name - dirname bytes (e.g. 9 bytes for "~username").

mempcpy copies end_name - dirname bytes from dirname + 1 into newp. This copies "username" (8 bytes) but the count is 9, so it copies one extra byte (the '/' character), filling all 9 bytes of the buffer.

state.value: end_name - dirname (9 bytes, which is username length + 1)

step_by_step_execution.description: The null terminator is written at the position returned by mempcpy, which is newp + (end_name - dirname), i.e. one byte past the end of the allocated buffer. This is a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: newp + 9, which is 1 byte past the 9-byte buffer

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2017-15670: an off-by-one error in glob's tilde/username processing where mempcpy copies one byte too many, causing a null terminator to be written past the allocated buffer. The fix changes `end_name - dirname` to `end_name - dirname - 1`.

actual_issue_summary: Off-by-one error in glob() when processing ~username with a trailing path component. The buffer for user_name is allocated as (end_name - dirname) bytes, but mempcpy copies (end_name - dirname) bytes from dirname+1, which is one byte too many since dirname+1 skips the '~' but the size still includes it. The null terminator is then written one byte past the buffer. The fix changes the mempcpy length to (end_name - dirname - 1).

cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel Intel’s iSMT SMBus host controller driver in the way it handled the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case (via the ioctl I2C_SMBUS) with malicious input data. This flaw could allow a local user to crash the system.

finding.description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case, data->block[0] is used directly as the size for memcpy without any bounds validation. If data->block[0] exceeds the size of the DMA buffer (which is derived from priv->buffer), this causes an out-of-bounds write into kernel memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: User triggers I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL via ioctl with data->block[0] set to a value greater than I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX (e.g., 255).
The code enters the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case without validating data->block[0].
memcpy copies data->block[0] bytes from data->block[1] into dma_buffer[1], overflowing the DMA buffer.

state.value: points to aligned priv->buffer, limited size

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-3077: the missing bounds check on data->block[0] in the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case allows a buffer overflow via memcpy. The fix adds the check `if (data->block[0] > I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX) return -EINVAL;`.

actual_issue_summary: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case of ismt_access(), data->block[0] (user-controlled, 0-255) was used without bounds checking as the length for memcpy into dma_buffer, which could write beyond the end of the buffer. The fix adds a check to return -EINVAL if data->block[0] > I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX.

cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

finding.description: Integer overflow in octet count parsing can lead to a negative iOctetsRemain value. When this negative value is later used as the size for memcpy (cast to size_t, becoming a very large unsigned value), it causes a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: An attacker sends a TCP message with octet-counting framing, starting with a very large digit sequence as the octet count.
The parser enters eInOctetCnt state and begins accumulating digits into iOctetsRemain without any upper bound check.
With enough digits, iOctetsRemain overflows the int type, wrapping to a negative value.
When the SP delimiter is received, the negative iOctetsRemain passes the < 1 check but only logs an error without aborting. inputState transitions to eInMsg.
In the TCP_FRAMING_OCTET_COUNTING branch, octatesToCopy is set to pThis->iOctetsRemain (negative), and when used in memcpy as size_t it becomes a huge value, causing a crash or heap buffer overflow.

state.value: negative int, cast to large size_t in memcpy

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the integer overflow in iOctetsRemain (CWE-190) that is the root cause of CVE-2018-16881. The fix adds a bound check (<=200000000) to prevent the overflow. The finding accurately describes how the overflow leads to a crash/DoS.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in the imptcp module's octet count parsing: pThis->iOctetsRemain could overflow when multiplied by 10 repeatedly without bounds checking, leading to negative values that cause a segmentation fault. The fix adds an upper bound check of 200000000 before the multiplication, and also handles edge cases for invalid octet counts.

cve_desc: The php_wddx_pop_element function in ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid base64 binary value, as demonstrated by a wddx_deserialize call that mishandles a binary element in a wddxPacket XML document.

finding.description: php_base64_decode can return NULL on failure. The old string is freed via STR_FREE, then the NULL pointer is stored as the zval's string value without any validation. Subsequent operations that access this string value (e.g., when the element is inserted into a hash table and later used) will dereference a NULL pointer, causing a crash or potential information disclosure.

step_by_step_execution.description: A wddxPacket XML document containing a binary element with invalid base64 data is deserialized via wddx_deserialize.
The function enters the EL_BINARY handling block and calls php_base64_decode on the invalid base64 data.
STR_FREE frees the old string value of ent1->data.
The NULL pointer from php_base64_decode is assigned to Z_STRVAL_P(ent1->data) without checking.
Later code that accesses this zval's string value dereferences the NULL pointer, causing a crash.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly describes CVE-2016-7130: php_base64_decode returns NULL for invalid base64 data, and the result is assigned to the zval without a NULL check, leading to a NULL pointer dereference. The fix adds exactly this NULL check.

actual_issue_summary: php_base64_decode can return NULL when given invalid base64 data in a binary element. The return value was assigned to the zval string without checking for NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference. The fix adds a NULL check and sets an empty string instead.

cve_desc: In OpenLDAP through 2.4.57 and 2.5.x through 2.5.1alpha, an assertion failure in slapd can occur in the issuerAndThisUpdateCheck function via a crafted packet, resulting in a denial of service (daemon exit) via a short timestamp. This is related to schema_init.c and checkTime.

finding.description: Similarly in the thisUpdate parsing path, if no closing double-quote is found within x.bv_len, the loop exits with tu->bv_len == x.bv_len and `x.bv_len -= tu->bv_len + 1` causes an unsigned integer underflow, leading to subsequent out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted packet is parsed in the thisUpdate branch

state.value: equal to x.bv_len if no closing quote found

step_by_step_execution.description: x.bv_len -= tu->bv_len + 1 causes unsigned underflow

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2021-27212) is about a short timestamp value in the thisUpdate field that passes through issuerAndThisUpdateCheck without length validation, then triggers an assertion failure in the downstream checkTime function. The fix adds `if ( tu->bv_len < STRLENOF("YYYYmmddHHmmssZ") ) return LDAP_INVALID_SYNTAX;` to reject short timestamps before they reach checkTime. The finding describes a different issue — an integer underflow when no closing quote is found — rather than the actual vulnerability of a short but syntactically valid timestamp causing an assertion failure (CWE-617) in checkTime. The described scenario, CWEs, and consequences don't match the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a missing length validation on the thisUpdate timestamp field in issuerAndThisUpdateCheck. A crafted packet with a short timestamp passes through this function and reaches the checkTime function, which contains an assertion that fails on timestamps shorter than expected (e.g., shorter than 'YYYYmmddHHmmssZ'), causing slapd to crash (denial of service). The fix adds a minimum length check on tu->bv_len before processing continues.

cve_desc: Curl versions 7.14.1 through 7.61.1 are vulnerable to a heap-based buffer over-read in the tool_msgs.c:voutf() function that may result in information exposure and denial of service.

finding.description: When the word-wrap search loop finds no space (cut reaches 0 and ptr[0] is not a space), cut=0. Then `len -= cut` means `len -= 0`, so len does not decrease. However, `ptr += cut+1` advances ptr by 1. This means len never decreases while ptr keeps advancing, causing the loop to read far beyond the buffer until len finally underflows or other memory corruption occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: User input is read into variable `x`

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the CVE-2018-16842 vulnerability. When a long word with no spaces is formatted, cut reaches 0, len never decreases (len -= 0), but ptr advances by 1 each iteration. This causes repeated reads past the end of the stack buffer print_buffer, which is exactly the heap/buffer over-read described in the CVE. The fix adds the check `if(0 == cut) cut = WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1` to handle this case.

actual_issue_summary: When warnf() processes a string with a word longer than WARN_TEXTWIDTH and no spaces are found during the word-wrap search, cut becomes 0. This causes len to never decrease (len -= 0) while ptr advances by 1 each iteration, leading to an out-of-bounds read past the print_buffer array.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.

finding.description: Heap buffer overflow: the buffer `skey` is allocated with `size` bytes (from the message header), but `skey->keylen` is read from the attacker-controlled message data and used as the length for `memcpy` into `skey->key` *before* the sanity check validates that `size == tipc_aead_key_size(skey)`. An attacker can craft a message where `keylen` exceeds the remaining space in the allocated buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write on the kernel heap.

step_by_step_execution.description: User input is read into variable `x`

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies CVE-2021-43267. The vulnerable code allocates `skey` based on `size` (from msg_data_sz), then reads `skey->keylen` from attacker-controlled message data and uses it as the memcpy length without prior validation. The sanity check occurs after the memcpy, so an attacker can supply a large keylen to overflow the heap buffer. The fix adds size and keylen validation before allocation and copy.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2021-43267: In tipc_crypto_key_rcv, the MSG_CRYPTO message type lacked proper validation of user-supplied sizes. The attacker-controlled `keylen` field was used in memcpy before being validated against the actual message size, enabling a heap buffer overflow. The fix adds upfront validation that the message size is sufficient and that keylen is consistent with the message size and within bounds.

cve_desc: Liblouis 3.21.0 has an out-of-bounds write in compileRule in compileTranslationTable.c, as demonstrated by lou_trace.

finding.description: The dynamically allocated 'arguments' array is never freed. On the success path (return 1) and on several error paths (e.g. when argument_count doesn't match, or when compileRule fails), the function returns without calling free(arguments), resulting in a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: malloc is called to allocate arguments array
Function returns without freeing arguments

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about an out-of-bounds write in the CTO_SeqAfterExpression case where ruleChars.length is not checked against SEQPATTERNSIZE before writing to seqAfterExpression. The fix adds this bounds check. The finding describes a memory leak in macro argument handling, which is a completely different issue unrelated to CVE-2022-31783.

actual_issue_summary: In the CTO_SeqAfterExpression case, ruleChars data was copied into (*table)->seqAfterExpression without checking if ruleChars.length exceeds the buffer size (SEQPATTERNSIZE). The fix adds a bounds check to prevent an out-of-bounds write.

cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the simplestring_addn function in simplestring.c in xmlrpc-epi through 0.54.2, as used in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a long first argument to the PHP xmlrpc_encode_request function.

finding.description: The `add_len` parameter is a signed `int`. If a negative value is passed, the size check `target->len + add_len + 1 > target->size` will evaluate to false (no realloc occurs), but `memcpy` implicitly converts the negative `add_len` to a very large `size_t`, causing a massive heap buffer overflow. Similarly, if `target->len + add_len + 1` overflows the `int` range to a negative value, the reallocation check is bypassed, leading to a heap buffer overflow when memcpy writes past the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: simplestring_addn is called with a large input that results in add_len being a large or negative int value

state.value: negative int or value causing integer overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: The size check `target->len + add_len + 1 > target->size` is bypassed because the signed arithmetic produces a small or negative result

state.value: negative or small value due to signed overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: No reallocation occurs, buffer remains at its current small size
memcpy is called with add_len implicitly converted to size_t, resulting in a massive write past the heap buffer

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly describes CVE-2016-6296: the integer signedness error in the `add_len` parameter (int vs size_t) that allows bypassing the size check and causing a heap-based buffer overflow via memcpy. The fix changes `add_len` from `int` to `size_t` and adds overflow checks, exactly matching this finding.

actual_issue_summary: Integer signedness error in simplestring_addn where the `add_len` parameter was declared as signed `int` instead of `size_t`. A negative or overflowing value could bypass the buffer size check, leading to a heap-based buffer overflow in the subsequent memcpy call. The fix changes `add_len` to `size_t` and adds explicit overflow checks.

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.2.

finding.description: Out-of-bounds read when reading `number_of_classes` from `buffer` at offset 6 without verifying that `sz` is large enough (at least 8 bytes). If `sz` is less than 8, the `R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT` macro reads 2 bytes past the end of the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a buffer where sz is small (e.g. less than 8 bytes)
offset is incremented to 6 after r_bin_java_default_attr_new
R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT reads 2 bytes at buffer[6..7] which is beyond the buffer size

judgments.reasoning: The fix adds exactly this check: `if (buf_offset + offset + 8 > sz) return NULL;` before the R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT read at offset 6. This is a direct component of the CVE — the missing bounds check leads to heap-based buffer overflow/OOB read. The CWE differs (CWE-125 vs CWE-787) but the underlying issue is the same missing bounds check.

actual_issue_summary: Missing bounds check before reading from buffer at offset 6 (number_of_classes field), and incorrect bounds check inside the loop that omitted buf_offset, both allowing out-of-bounds heap buffer access when parsing malformed Java class files.

cve_desc: libmobi is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Write

finding.description: When offset is negative, the code only checks that `buf->offset >= aoffset` (to prevent source pointer underflow), but it never validates that `buf->offset + len <= buf->maxlen` or that the source region `(buf->offset - aoffset + len)` stays within the buffer. This allows an out-of-bounds read from `source` and an out-of-bounds write to the destination via `memmove`.

step_by_step_execution.description: mobi_buffer_move is called with a negative offset and a large len value

state.value: large value such that buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen

step_by_step_execution.description: aoffset is computed as abs(offset); since offset < 0, the else branch is taken
The check `buf->offset < aoffset` passes (offset is not too large), but no check is made for buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen
memmove writes len bytes starting at buf->data + buf->offset, exceeding the buffer boundary

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly describes the missing bounds check for `buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen` in the negative offset branch, which is precisely what the fix adds. This matches CVE-2021-3751 (out-of-bounds write in libmobi).

actual_issue_summary: When offset is negative, mobi_buffer_move only checked that the source pointer wouldn't underflow (buf->offset < aoffset) but did not check that buf->offset + len stays within buf->maxlen, allowing an out-of-bounds write via memmove. The fix adds the missing `buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen` check.

cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in jetp3852_print_page() in devices/gdev3852.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to escalate privileges via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

finding.description: Stack buffer overflow: when `end_data` points near or at the end of the `data` buffer (i.e., `data + DATA_SIZE`), `memset(end_data, 0, 7)` writes up to 7 bytes beyond the `data` array. The check only ensures `line_size <= DATA_SIZE`, but the padding write is not bounded by the buffer's end.

step_by_step_execution.description: data[] is declared as a stack buffer of size DATA_SIZE
line_size is computed; it can be up to DATA_SIZE
end_data is set to data + line_size
Trailing zeros are removed by moving end_data backwards, but if the last bytes are non-zero, end_data remains at data + DATA_SIZE
memset(end_data, 0, 7) writes 7 bytes starting from end_data, which is past the end of the buffer

state.value: data[DATA_SIZE] through data[DATA_SIZE+6], which is beyond the stack buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies a buffer overflow component of CVE-2020-16302. The `memset(end_data, 0, 7)` can write up to 7 bytes beyond the `data` stack buffer when `end_data` is at or near `data + DATA_SIZE`. This problematic `memset` was removed in the fix and replaced with a full buffer initialization (`memset(data, 0x00, DATA_SIZE)`) at the start. Additionally, the uninitialised bytes beyond `line_size` (but within DATA_SIZE) could have values > 7, causing out-of-bounds reads on the spr* lookup tables. The finding captures a real part of the vulnerability that was fixed.

actual_issue_summary: The `data` buffer was not fully initialized, so bytes beyond `line_size` but within `DATA_SIZE` could contain arbitrary values. These uninitialised bytes were used as indices into fixed-size (8-element) lookup arrays `spr40[]`, `spr8[]`, `spr2[]`, causing out-of-bounds reads when values exceeded 7. The old `memset(end_data, 0, 7)` only zeroed 7 bytes of padding (and could itself overflow the buffer). The fix initializes the entire `data` buffer to zero upfront and removes the inadequate 7-byte memset.

cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

finding.description: In the match-offset-wrapping case (match_offset > window_posn), the copy length `i` (initialized to `match_length`) is never clamped against the remaining space in the window (`window_size - window_posn`). This allows writing past the end of the `window` buffer. The non-wrapping else branch has this clamp, but the wrapping branch does not.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted CAB archive using Quantum compression is processed by qtm_decompress.
A match is decoded with match_length large enough that window_posn + match_length > window_size.

state.value: large enough to overflow past window end

step_by_step_execution.description: rundest is set to &window[window_posn], and i is set to match_length without any bounds check.
The copy loop writes past the end of the window buffer, corrupting memory.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the missing bounds check that the fix adds: `if (window_posn + match_length > qtm->window_size)`. Without this check, a crafted CAB archive can cause writes beyond the window buffer, leading to memory corruption and crash — exactly matching CVE-2010-1311.

actual_issue_summary: The Quantum decompressor in qtm_decompress lacked a check that window_posn + match_length does not exceed window_size before performing match copy operations. A crafted CAB archive could trigger an out-of-bounds write past the window buffer, causing memory corruption and application crash.

cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

finding.description: If dl->length is smaller than so_ext_len (sizeof(SO_EXT) - 1), the expression `dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len` computes a pointer that points before the start of the dl->str buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds read (buffer under-read) when my_strcasecmp dereferences it.

step_by_step_execution.description: fix_dl_name is called with a dl whose length is smaller than so_ext_len
The pointer dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len underflows, pointing before dl->str
my_strcasecmp_8bit dereferences the invalid pointer, reading out-of-bounds memory

state.value: out-of-bounds / use-after-free depending on allocator state

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the root cause fixed by the patch: missing bounds check on dl->length before computing dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len. When dl->length < so_ext_len, the pointer underflows and my_strcasecmp_8bit reads invalid memory. The CVE describes this as use-after-free in my_strcasecmp_8bit; the finding describes it as out-of-bounds read. Both describe the same underlying bug — the missing length check — and the same code path. The CWE difference (CWE-125 vs CWE-416) is a minor classification difference for the same issue.

actual_issue_summary: When dl->length is smaller than so_ext_len, the expression `dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len` underflows, causing my_strcasecmp_8bit to access memory before the buffer. The fix adds a `dl->length < so_ext_len` check before the pointer arithmetic.

cve_desc: A buffer overflow in the GmfOpenMesh() function of libMeshb v7.61 allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted MESH file.

finding.description: The function reads strings from the file using fscanf with "%s" format specifier into a fixed-size stack buffer `str[GmfStrSiz]` without any length limit. A crafted mesh file containing a token longer than GmfStrSiz bytes (before any whitespace) will cause a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: GmfOpenMesh is called with a crafted .mesh file for reading.
The file is detected as ASCII mesh (.mesh extension), and opened for reading.
fscanf(msh->hdl, "%s", str) is called in the do-while loop searching for "MeshVersionFormatted". The "%s" specifier has no width limit.

state.value: buffer of GmfStrSiz bytes on the stack

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted file contains a token longer than GmfStrSiz bytes without whitespace, causing fscanf to write beyond the bounds of str.

state.value: overflowed past GmfStrSiz bytes, corrupting adjacent stack memory

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches the CVE. The fix changed "%s" to "%100s" in both fscanf calls to limit the read length, preventing the buffer overflow in the stack-allocated str buffer.

actual_issue_summary: Two fscanf calls in GmfOpenMesh used "%s" format specifier to read into a fixed-size buffer `str[GmfStrSiz]` with no length limit, allowing a crafted MESH file with an oversized token to overflow the buffer. The fix added a width specifier ("%100s") to both fscanf calls.

cve_desc: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 does not check a certain return value from the read_mapping_page function before calling kmap, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (system crash) via a crafted hfsplus filesystem image.

finding.description: read_mapping_page() can return an ERR_PTR (error-encoded pointer) on I/O failure or memory pressure. The return value is never checked with IS_ERR() before being passed to kmap() and dereferenced. This leads to dereferencing an invalid kernel pointer, causing a kernel crash/oops (denial of service or worse).

step_by_step_execution.description: read_mapping_page() is called with a crafted hfsplus filesystem image
The error pointer is passed directly to kmap() without IS_ERR() check
kmap() dereferences the invalid pointer, causing a kernel oops/crash

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2008-4934: the return value of read_mapping_page() is not checked with IS_ERR() before being passed to kmap(), allowing a crafted hfsplus image to cause a kernel crash. The fix adds IS_ERR() checks at all three call sites.

actual_issue_summary: hfsplus_block_allocate() passes the return value of read_mapping_page() directly to kmap() at three locations without checking if it's an error pointer (IS_ERR). A crafted hfsplus filesystem image can trigger read_mapping_page() failure, returning an ERR_PTR that causes a kernel oops when dereferenced by kmap().

cve_desc: This affects the package juce-framework/JUCE before 6.1.5. This vulnerability is triggered when a malicious archive is crafted with an entry containing a symbolic link. When extracted, the symbolic link is followed outside of the target dir allowing writing arbitrary files on the target host. In some cases, this can allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. The vulnerable code is in the ZipFile::uncompressEntry function in juce_ZipFile.cpp and is executed when the archive is extracted upon calling uncompressTo() on a ZipFile object.

finding.description: The entry filename from the zip archive is used directly in getChildFile without sanitization. A malicious zip can contain entries with path traversal sequences such as '../../etc/cron.d/malicious', causing files to be written outside the intended target directory (Zip Slip attack).

step_by_step_execution.description: The entry path is read from the zip archive entry
targetFile is computed by joining targetDirectory with the malicious entryPath
No check is performed to verify targetFile is within targetDirectory
File is written to the attacker-controlled location

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-23521) describes writing arbitrary files outside the target directory when extracting a malicious zip. The fix adds `isAChildOf` check to prevent path traversal AND a symlink check. The finding correctly identifies the missing path traversal validation, which is a core component of the CVE. While the CVE description emphasizes symbolic links, path traversal via crafted entry names is part of the same vulnerability class addressed by the fix (the `isAChildOf` check).

actual_issue_summary: The ZipFile::uncompressEntry function lacks validation that the target file path stays within the target directory. A malicious zip archive can contain entries with path traversal sequences or symbolic links that cause files to be written outside the intended extraction directory. The fix adds an `isAChildOf` check to ensure the target file is within the target directory, and a symlink check to prevent following symbolic links in parent directories.
